title: US Pushes Ahead With $700M+ Jet Engine Sale to Turkey as Trump Signals F-35 Reentry
subhead: The Trump administration is moving to finalize a major jet engine sale to Turkey despite Congressional objections, while Trump personally signaled Turkey could rejoin the F-35 program — all days before Erdogan expects bilateral talks at next month's NATO summit in Ankara. Meanwhile, Erdogan announced a legal framework to accelerate PKK disbandment and Ankara detained 209 in security sweeps ahead of the summit.

sections.heading: US-Turkey Jet Engine Sale Moves Forward Despite Congressional Hold
sections.body:
The Trump administration is planning to push ahead with the sale of dozens of General Electric F-110 jet engines to Turkey worth over $700 million, according to four sources familiar with the matter (Reuters, Al-Monitor). The engines are destined for Turkey's indigenous Kaan combat jet program, launched in 2016 as part of Ankara's push for defense self-sufficiency.

Rep. Gregory Meeks (D-NY), ranking Democrat on the House Foreign Affairs Committee, has raised objections during the informal review process and has not given his green light, citing Turkey's continued possession of the Russian S-400 air defense system. Despite that hold, the sale is expected to be finalized in the coming days, followed by a formal State Department notification to Congress (Reuters, WSJ/LiveMint).

The move represents a rare executive override of the informal congressional review process for arms sales. As the LiveMint analysis notes, it underscores the limits of the personal Trump-Erdogan rapport — while warm, it has not resolved the 'structural problem' of the S-400, which still clouds the broader defense relationship (Carnegie's Alper Coskun, quoted in LiveMint).
sections.whyItMatters: The engine sale is the clearest signal yet that Washington is willing to prioritize alliance management over congressional concerns ahead of the NATO summit. But bypassing the Meeks hold sets a precedent and does not resolve the underlying S-400 dispute.

sections.heading: Trump Signals Turkey Could Rejoin F-35 Program
sections.body:
At a White House meeting with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte on Wednesday, President Trump was asked whether he would bring good news to Ankara regarding Turkey's F-35 participation. He replied: 'I think so,' adding: 'I'm going to probably do something that's going to make them very happy' (DPA/Yahoo News).

Vice President JD Vance confirmed a review was underway to determine whether Turkey meets the requirements for renewed participation in the F-35 program, describing the process as still ongoing. Trump also praised his relationship with Erdogan, calling him 'a very good person' and suggesting he might not even attend the July 7-8 NATO summit in Ankara were it not for the Turkish leader.

Turkey was removed from the F-35 program in 2019 after purchasing the Russian S-400 system, and subsequent CAATSA sanctions were imposed on its defense procurement agency. Any reentry would require resolving the S-400 compatibility question that originally prompted Turkey's exclusion.
sections.whyItMatters: A potential F-35 reentry would be the most significant reversal in US-Turkey defense relations in years — but it remains conditional on the unresolved S-400 issue, and Trump's remarks are more signal than commitment at this stage.

sections.heading: Erdogan Announces Legal Framework to Accelerate PKK Disbandment
sections.body:
President Erdogan told his AKP parliamentary group on Wednesday that a legal framework to accelerate the PKK's liquidation process is being prepared and will soon be presented to parliament. 'We are working on a legal framework that will accelerate the liquidation process of the PKK,' Erdogan said. 'After holding the necessary consultations, we will present the regulation in question to the discretion of parliament without much delay' (Reuters, Bianet).

Erdogan acknowledged that the process had slowed due to regional developments, specifically the Iran war and concerns it triggered about further instability. He welcomed the integration of Syrian Kurdish forces into Syria's interim government — reached in January after Kurdish-led groups lost Arab-majority territory — calling it 'one of the biggest obstacles in front of the process' that had been removed.

The peace process was initiated after MHP leader Devlet Bahceli suggested in October 2024 that imprisoned PKK leader Abdullah Ocalan could be released in exchange for the PKK dissolving itself. The PKK declared its dissolution at a congress in May 2025 and held a symbolic disarmament ceremony. A parliamentary commission completed its work on the legal framework in December 2025, but no legislation had been introduced until now (Bianet).
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first concrete legislative step after months of stagnation. The framework's scope — whether it includes amnesty provisions, reintegration, or Ocalan's status — will determine whether the process moves forward or stalls again.

sections.heading: Ankara Detains 209 in Anti-Terror Raids Ahead of NATO Summit
sections.body:
Turkish authorities detained 209 people in anti-terrorism operations on Tuesday, prosecutors said, a day after Ankara imposed restrictions on public gatherings ahead of the July 7-8 NATO summit (Reuters). The raids targeted individuals suspected of links to militant organizations, though specific groups were not named in the initial reporting.

The public gathering restrictions and mass detentions signal the scale of security preparations for the summit, which will host 32 NATO leaders as well as representatives from the Istanbul Cooperation Initiative and Indo-Pacific Four partners (Japan, South Korea, Australia, New Zealand) at the foreign ministers' level (Al-Monitor).
sections.whyItMatters: The security crackdown is standard pre-summit procedure but also serves as a reminder of the domestic political space the Erdogan government is willing to compress to ensure a smooth diplomatic showcase.

sections.heading: Erdogan Expects Bilateral Talks With Trump at NATO Summit
sections.body:
President Erdogan told reporters outside parliament on Wednesday that he would 'most likely' hold bilateral talks with Trump during the July 7-8 NATO summit in Ankara. He did not elaborate on the agenda (Reuters, Al-Monitor).

Erdogan's communications director Burhanettin Duran said burden-sharing would be among key agenda points, noting that Turkey aims to reach the 3.5% + 1.5% defense spending target by the end of 2030. The summit takes place amid alliance tensions over burden-sharing, defense spending, and US complaints about allies' role in keeping the Strait of Hormuz open during the Iran war (Al-Monitor).

Separately, NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte met Trump at the White House on Wednesday to defuse tensions ahead of the summit. According to the New York Times, Rutte used charts to show Trump that European countries were increasing their defense spending — an effort that appeared to ease the president's frustrations about alliance burden-sharing (NYT).
sections.whyItMatters: The Erdogan-Trump bilateral will set the tone for whether the jet engine sale and F-35 signals translate into concrete deliverables — or remain gestures. The 3.5% + 1.5% spending commitment is Turkey's answer to the burden-sharing debate, but the Hormuz question remains a live friction point.

sections.heading: Central Bank Holds Rate at 37%, Inflation Expectations Contained
sections.body:
Turkey's central bank left its key interest rate at 37% for a third consecutive meeting on June 11, as expected (Reuters). The bank cited elevated energy prices from the Iran conflict as a factor keeping inflation pressures alive (WSJ).

Central bank governor Fatih Karahan said deterioration in inflation expectations remains limited despite the geopolitical backdrop, noting that lira demand remains robust and FX reserves are strong (Anadolu Agency). May inflation came in at 1.7% monthly and 32.6% annually, slightly above expectations, reflecting the pass-through from energy costs linked to the Iran war (Reuters).
sections.whyItMatters: The CBRT is holding firm at 37% and signaling confidence in the lira and reserve position — but the Iran war's energy price channel remains the main inflation risk, and any escalation could force a tightening pivot.

sections.heading: Europe's Defense Spending Push Sidelines Turkey at Its Peril
sections.body:
As Europe ramps up defense spending through the 150-billion-euro SAFE instrument, Turkey finds itself politically shut out of the procurement framework despite being the world's 11th-largest arms exporter, with five firms in the Defense News Top 100 and 2024 defense exports hitting a record $7.1 billion (SIPRI, Forbes/Guney Yildiz). Five Turkish firms now sit in the global defense top 100 (Anadolu Agency).

France has pushed to block SAFE funds from being used for Turkish weapons, including for Ukraine, while Germany has argued for inclusion. The result is that European nations are bypassing the EU mechanism entirely, engaging in bilateral deals with both the US and Turkey for urgently needed items like 155mm shells and drones — creating what Forbes terms a 'complicated dependency' where Europe pays more for less capability (Forbes).

Separately, Italy conditionally approved the Leonardo-Baykar drone joint venture on June 17, with restrictions on exports and technology sharing under Italy's golden power rules (Reuters). Turkey's defense exports have tripled since 2021 to $10 billion, and officials plan to showcase the sector at the July NATO summit in Ankara (Reuters).
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey has the production capacity Europe urgently needs, but political friction — especially over the S-400 and human rights concerns — keeps it out of formal EU channels. The NATO summit is Turkey's chance to reframe itself as an indispensable defense partner, but the bilateral deals happening anyway suggest the market is already solving around the politics.

bottomLine: A single day brought three major US-Turkey defense signals: the $700M+ jet engine sale moving past a congressional hold, Trump personally hinting at F-35 reentry, and Erdogan expecting a bilateral with Trump at next month's Ankara summit. The engine sale is real and imminent; the F-35 talk is still conditional. On the domestic side, Erdogan announced the long-awaited legal framework to advance PKK disbandment — the first concrete legislative step after months of stagnation — while 209 were detained in summit security sweeps. Economically, the CBRT holds at 37% with contained expectations but Iran-war energy pressures as the key risk. The common thread: everything converges on the July 7-8 NATO summit, which Ankara is treating as both a security operation and a strategic showcase for Turkey's renewed relevance.
sources: Reuters, Al-Monitor, Bianet, Devdiscourse, Anadolu Agency, WSJ, LiveMint/WSJ, Forbes/Guney Yildiz, DPA/Yahoo News, SIPRI, NYT
